text: This is the formation of a cock pouch or continent ileostomy in a sixty four year old man who had had rectal cancer and had previously undergone a robotic TME and ileostomy. He later developed local recurrence which was managed with an abdominal perineal excision and end colostomy but unfortunately he suffered from several stoma complications and was keen to have a continent stoma with the benefits of no longer necessitating wearing a stoma bag and reduced ileostomy complications.

The surgeon approaches the abdomen through a midline laparotomy incision, revealing the previous side-to-side stapled anastomosis from the ileostomy closure. The mesenteric vessels are sealed and divided using a ligature, and the terminal ileum is stapled and divided just proximal to the ileocecal valve.

The surgeon carefully measures fifteen centimeters from the transected terminal ileum to ensure adequate small bowel length for the formation of the nipple valve and continent ileostomy. Additional fifteen-centimeter segments are measured for each of the three limbs of the S pouch. Sutures are placed to mark the top of the pouch and each limb, with adjustments made to correct any discrepancies in length.

The surgeon positions the first and second, and the second and third limbs of the small bowel adjacent to each other, joining them with a continuous 3-0 vicryl seromuscular suture. Diathermy is used to mark the incision lines along each limb on either side of the suture line. The small bowel is then incised along these lines and laid open to maximize the capacity of the pouch.
The surgeon opens each limb of the pouch sequentially, taking care to avoid damaging the posterior wall of the lumen and ensuring that the sutures are not disturbed, especially when turning corners.

The surgeon sutures the adjacent posterior walls of the opened loops of ileum together using a continuous three ovicle suture, employing full thickness stitches. The posterior wall is formed by joining two adjacent limbs, followed by suturing the second and third limbs.

The surgeon forms the anterior wall of the pouch by suturing the lateral edges of the first and third limbs of the S pouch. Stay sutures are visible at either end and in the middle of the anterior wall. The lower half of the pouch is joined with a continuous suture, while the top half remains open for nipple valve formation.

The surgeon uses diathermy to thin out the mesenteric fat bordering the terminal ileum, facilitating its interception for nipple valve formation. A diathermy scratch pad is applied to rub the serosal surface of the terminal ileum, promoting adhesion between the loops of interseceptive bowel. Dry powdered doxycycline is also used to enhance tissue granulation and prevent slippage of the interception.

The surgeon rubs the terminal ileum to promote adhesion between visceral layers, similar to pleurodesis, enhancing the site for nipple valve formation. The peritoneum over the mesentery is carefully incised at the intended site for the nipple valve. Attempts are made to intercept the ileum, but challenges arise due to bulky mesentery and narrow bowel lumen, necessitating excision of adjacent fat.

The surgeon carefully applies traction to successfully intercept the terminal ileum, forming the interception. A soft silastic drain is then inserted from the distal ileum through the newly formed nipple valve and into the top of the pouch.

The surgeon sutures the rolled edge of the ileum using interrupted serosal sutures. A Reticulator-Fifty-Five stapler with a green 4.8mm cartridge is then applied, placing a double staggered row of titanium staples along the tissue.

The surgeon uses a stapler to fire lactamir copolymer staples at three separate points around the inter-suscepted small bowel. The top of the anterior wall of the pouch is then closed around the nipple valve using a series of interrupted sutures to minimize the risk of leakage at this potentially weak point.

The surgeon places further interrupted sutures to close the top of the pouch and along the anterior wall. The nipple valve and inserted tube are visible within the pouch. Some anterior wall sutures anchor the inter-susceptive valve to the nipple valve. The Reticulator stapler is inserted within the pouch and into the end of the nipple valve, followed by careful withdrawal of the drain. A final firing of the stapler secures the nipple valve to the anterior wall of the pouch, completing the anastomosis.

The surgeon tests the pouch for leaks by filling it with normal saline, checking its capacity. The patency of the nipple valve is assessed through repeated catheterization, involving the removal and reinsertion of the thylastic drain to ensure adequate drainage.

The surgeon inserts a second layer of interrupted sutures along the anterior anastomosis using Threoethabond. A small skin incision, measuring 1.5 to 2 centimeters, is made in the lower right iliac fossa and extended through the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon inserts a small Alexis wound protector to aid in the exteriorization of the terminal ileum. The pouch is visually checked to ensure it is not twisted and is correctly oriented. The drain is used to confirm the patency of the nipple valve, and the pouch is filled with normal saline. The ileostomy is re-catheterized to verify adequate drainage.

The surgeon confirms the continence of the ileostomy by removing the drain when the pouch is full. The excess ileum is excised, and the ileostomy is formed flush with the skin, secured with a series of interrupted vial stitches.

The drain is positioned through the ileostomy, passing through the valve and into the pouch, and is secured with a silk stitch. The surgical field shows the completed colectomy and closure of the previous colostomy site.
